name: Memory Protection Keys — MPK / PKU / Arm POE
summary: 4-bit (x86) or 3-bit (ARM POE) key per page table entry plus a per-thread permission register; permission changes via single user-mode instruction (~20–26 cycles), no TLB flush. Intra-process isolation without page-table modifications; per-thread view enables data-plane sandboxing.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
4-bit (x86) or 3-bit (ARM POE) key per page table entry + per-thread permission register (PKRU/POR_EL0); permission changes via single user-mode WRPKRU/MSR write (~20–26 cycles), *no TLB flush*. Intra-process isolation without page-table modifications; per-thread view enables data-plane sandboxing (libmpk, ERIM, Hodor).

The fast cross-domain switch (no TLB flush) is what makes MPK uniquely useful for in-process compartmentalisation — orthogonal to the per-pointer mechanisms (MTE, PAC, CHERI capabilities).

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content:
Intel Skylake-SP (2017)+ server, Tiger Lake+ client; AMD Zen 3+; ARMv8.9 FEAT_S1POE (2024+ silicon). Linux pkey APIs upstream. Used in WebAssembly sandboxes, OpenSSL key isolation, JIT W^X.

MPK is the most mainstream of the in-process compartmentalisation hardware — much more deployed than CHERI capabilities, and unlike software-only tricks (separate processes, mprotect-on-context-switch) it has the cycle budget to be used per-call rather than per-syscall.

sources.label: Connor et al. — MPK security (USENIX Security 2020)
Connor et al. — MPK security (USENIX Security 2020)
